parsed_findings.chain_steps: Single-step: inject via `dir` into the dynamically built ORDER BY clause.
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker-controlled SQL fragments after ORDER BY may enable data theft or destructive statements depending on the DB and driver.
parsed_findings.proof: `direction` comes from `request.args.get('dir','asc')` and is inserted into the query string alongside the whitelisted `sort`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Issue a request setting `sort=name` and a malicious `dir` value crafted for the backend SQL dialect.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The user-controlled `dir` value is concatenated into SQL without validation while only `sort` is allowlisted.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-89","file":"app.py","line_start":7,"line_end":7,"vuln_claim":"The user-controlled `dir` value is concatenated into SQL without validation while only `sort` is allowlisted.","proof":"`direction` comes from `request.args.get('dir','asc')` and is inserted into the query string alongside the whitelisted `sort`.","repro_steps":"Issue a request setting `sort=name` and a malicious `dir` value crafted for the backend SQL dialect.","impact":"Attacker-controlled SQL fragments after ORDER BY may enable data theft or destructive statements depending on the DB and driver.","confidence":0.88,"chain_steps":"Single-step: inject via `dir` into the dynamically built ORDER BY clause."}]}

parsed_findings.impact: SSRF can expose internal services, cloud instance metadata, or other restricted network resources.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 3’s `//` begins a line comment, so the intended allowlist check is disabled and `axios.get(u)` on line 6 can target internal/metadata endpoints.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Issue `GET /fetch?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` (or another internal URL) and observe sensitive data returned in the response.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `url` is passed to `axios.get` and echoed, enabling server-side requests to arbitrary hosts.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Malformed client request directly triggers the exception on line 6.
parsed_findings.impact: Clients can reliably trigger server errors, reducing availability and possibly leaking stack traces depending on app config.
parsed_findings.proof: Expression `request.json.get(...)` dereferences `None` when JSON parsing yields no object.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Issue `PATCH /api/invoices/<id>` with a non-JSON body or missing JSON so `request.json` is `None`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `request.json` can be `None`, so calling `.get` raises `AttributeError` and aborts the handler.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Invalid identifier yields `None` invoice then immediate exception on authorization check.
parsed_findings.impact: Predictable 500 errors enable denial-of-service against the route and may expose error details.
parsed_findings.proof: After `inv = db.get_invoice(inv_id)`, `inv.project_id` assumes `inv` is always an object.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call the endpoint with an `inv_id` that resolves to no invoice if the DB helper returns `None`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: If `db.get_invoice` returns `None` for an unknown `inv_id`, accessing `inv.project_id` dereferences `None`.

parsed_findings.impact: Unauthenticated arbitrary filesystem read outside the intended data directory.
parsed_findings.proof: Node path.join('/srv/data','/etc/passwd') normalizes to '/etc/passwd'.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request GET /download?file=/etc/passwd and observe sensitive file contents returned.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: path.join discards the /srv/data prefix when the joined segment is an absolute path, so the base directory is not enforced.

parsed_findings.impact: Path traversal enables reading unintended files relative to the configured root.
parsed_findings.proof: '../../etc/passwd'.replace('../','') becomes '../etc/passwd', which still traverses upward when joined under /srv/data.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request GET /download?file=../../etc/passwd (or similar layered forms) and confirm read outside /srv/data.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: A single literal replace('../','') only strips the first occurrence and can be bypassed with residual '../' sequences or obfuscated dot-slash forms.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-22","file":"files.js","line_start":3,"line_end":3,"vuln_claim":"path.join discards the /srv/data prefix when the joined segment is an absolute path, so the base directory is not enforced.","proof":"Node path.join('/srv/data','/etc/passwd') normalizes to '/etc/passwd'.","repro_steps":"Request GET /download?file=/etc/passwd and observe sensitive file contents returned.","impact":"Unauthenticated arbitrary filesystem read outside the intended data directory.","confidence":0.95,"chain_steps":""},{"cwe":"CWE-22","file":"files.js","line_start":2,"line_end":2,"vuln_claim":"A single literal replace('../','') only strips the first occurrence and can be bypassed with residual '../' sequences or obfuscated dot-slash forms.","proof":"'../../etc/passwd'.replace('../','') becomes '../etc/passwd', which still traverses upward when joined under /srv/data.","repro_steps":"Request GET /download?file=../../etc/passwd (or similar layered forms) and confirm read outside /srv/data.","impact":"Path traversal enables reading unintended files relative to the configured root.","confidence":0.9,"chain_steps":""}]}

parsed_findings.impact: Remote code execution in the server process handling the request.
parsed_findings.proof: `pickle.loads` on attacker-controlled bytes can trigger arbitrary callables via crafted pickle opcodes.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Base64-encode a malicious pickle and send `Cookie: state=<payload>` to `GET /resume`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The `state` cookie is unpickled, letting attackers run code during deserialization.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Mass-assign elevated role/permissions, then use normal app flows with escalated rights.
parsed_findings.impact: Privilege escalation or unauthorized modification of protected account attributes.
parsed_findings.proof: A client can supply additional JSON keys that map to sensitive ORM/database columns and have them saved with the user record.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: PATCH /api/users/:id with a JSON body containing privileged fields (e.g., role/flags) and observe they persist on the target user.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Object.assign copies attacker-controlled req.body properties onto the loaded user object before persistence without field allowlisting.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Broken/missing guard permits updating arbitrary user ids combined with unsafe body merge.
parsed_findings.impact: Either the route fails to load or intended restrictions on PATCH /api/users/:id are not enforced as designed.
parsed_findings.proof: As written, the if-condition is not valid JavaScript and cannot implement a reliable same-user/support-only update check.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Load or parse users.js and observe the invalid if-condition on line 2 preventing correct authorization logic.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The authorization guard expression is malformed (missing operators between req.user.id and req.user.isSupport), so it cannot correctly enforce intended access rules.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: create_comment stores unsafe html; list_comments returns it for client-side HTML rendering.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can run script in other users browsers when comments are displayed, leading to session abuse or defacement.
parsed_findings.proof: Python-Markdown preserves inline HTML by default, so tags like img onerror or script survive in rendered and in db.insert_comment html.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /comments with body containing raw HTML or markdown mixing HTML, then serve that html to a browser as HTML.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User body is passed through markdown.markdown and stored as HTML without sanitization, allowing stored XSS.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Single-request logic flaw; no chaining required.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker can inflate their own balance and steal funds from arbitrary accounts.
parsed_findings.proof: A negative amount makes the insufficient-funds check pass while line 8 adds funds to the sender and line 9 subtracts from the recipient.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /transfer with JSON containing a negative integer amount and a victim user id in to.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The signed integer amount is never constrained to be non-negative, so debits become credits and the payee is debited instead.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Parallel requests exploit non-atomic check-then-act; no prerequisite bugs.
parsed_findings.impact: The account can be overdrawn or the ledger can become inconsistent, enabling theft or denial of service.
parsed_findings.proof: Two requests can both observe sufficient balance before either write completes, causing combined debits to exceed the original balance.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Issue two parallel POST /transfer requests each for nearly the full balance before responses return.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The balance check and the two balance writes are not performed as one atomic transaction, enabling a concurrent double-spend race.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Standalone: verification error path substitutes unverified token material for verified output.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can impersonate users or elevate privileges by supplying forged tokens that fail verify but decode successfully.
parsed_findings.proof: jwt.decode parses claims without verifying the signature, so any tampered JWT that fails verify still yields attacker-controlled payload objects.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Run with NODE_ENV=production, send a syntactically valid JWT with an invalid signature in Authorization, and observe verifyToken returning decoded claims.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Failed RS256 verification in production returns jwt.decode results, which are not cryptographically authenticated.

parsed_findings.impact: Authenticated attackers can read other users' emails via predictable id parameters (IDOR).
parsed_findings.proof: After login_required, line 7 binds uid from the request into SELECT id,email FROM users WHERE id = %s and line 8 returns that row.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Authenticate, send GET /u?id=<victim_id>, and observe the victim's id/email returned.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The handler uses client-supplied uid to fetch and return a user row without verifying uid matches the authenticated principal.

parsed_findings.impact: A sufficiently precise attacker may reduce brute-force/search space for the shared secret or valid MAC.
parsed_findings.proof: Non-constant-time string comparison can correlate response latency with how many prefix bytes match the expected digest.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Issue many requests with crafted `X-Sig` values and statistically compare response times against a known-good signature.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The HMAC tag is checked with `==`, which is not constant-time and can leak comparison progress via timing.

parsed_findings.impact: Duplicate processing of the same event can corrupt idempotency and amplify privileged side effects.
parsed_findings.proof: Replaying an old successful request with the same `body` and `X-Sig` still satisfies `got == want` and reaches `process_event`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Capture one legitimate webhook, then send the identical bytes and `X-Sig` header again after a delay.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The MAC covers only the raw body, with no freshness token, so any captured valid `(body, X-Sig)` can be replayed.

parsed_findings.impact: Open redirect enables phishing, session fixation, and OAuth/code theft via trusted-domain links.
parsed_findings.proof: next='//evil.com' satisfies startswith('/') but redirect(Location: //evil.com) sends the browser to an external host.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send GET /go?next=//evil.com and follow the 302 Location header.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Path-only allowlist via startswith('/') misses protocol-relative targets that still begin with '/'.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Single-hop SSRF suffices for internal access without requiring additional bugs.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can abuse the server as a relay to scan or access internal networks and cloud instance metadata services.
parsed_findings.proof: requests.get follows attacker-chosen URLs such as http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/, causing the backend to retrieve restricted internal responses.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Physical/root file access or backup extraction to read shared_prefs, then reuse the stolen bearer token against the API.
parsed_findings.impact: Bearer token theft enables account/session takeover if an attacker can read app-private files via root, malware with elevated access, or misconfigured backups.
parsed_findings.proof: MODE_PRIVATE only blocks other apps, not rooted users, forensic imaging, or unsafe backup/export paths that can still recover the raw token file.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call saveToken with a known token, then inspect the app’s shared_prefs XML on a rooted device or backup and observe the token stored in plaintext.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The access token is written to SharedPreferences, which persists it as cleartext XML under the app’s private storage.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-312","file":"SessionStore.kt","line_start":3,"line_end":4,"vuln_claim":"The access token is written to SharedPreferences, which persists it as cleartext XML under the app’s private storage.","proof":"MODE_PRIVATE only blocks other apps, not rooted users, forensic imaging, or unsafe backup/export paths that can still recover the raw token file.","repro_steps":"Call saveToken with a known token, then inspect the app’s shared_prefs XML on a rooted device or backup and observe the token stored in plaintext.","impact":"Bearer token theft enables account/session takeover if an attacker can read app-private files via root, malware with elevated access, or misconfigured backups.","confidence":0.72,"chain_steps":"Physical/root file access or backup extraction to read shared_prefs, then reuse the stolen bearer token against the API."}]}

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Recover KEY from source or binary, then decrypt IV-prefixed CBC blobs offline.
parsed_findings.impact: Confidentiality of all data encrypted with enc() collapses once the static key is known.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 2 sets KEY = b'0123456789abcdef' and line 5 passes KEY into AES.new for every encryption.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Read line 2 to obtain KEY, parse the first 16 bytes of enc() output as IV and the remainder as ciphertext, then AES-CBC decrypt.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: A fixed AES key is embedded in source, so anyone with the code or binary can decrypt all ciphertexts produced by enc().

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Bit-flip CBC blocks in transit then exploit any decrypt-and-act logic that lacks integrity checks.
parsed_findings.impact: Decrypted data integrity and downstream security decisions relying on it may be subverted where decryption is exposed.
parsed_findings.proof: Lines 5-6 use AES.MODE_CBC and return only iv plus raw ciphertext with no authentication field.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Alter bytes in the ciphertext portion after IV and observe that a decrypting peer may accept modified plaintext if no MAC is checked.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: AES-CBC output lacks an integrity tag, so ciphertext is malleable unless the application adds a separate MAC or uses an AEAD.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Inverted null check on line 3 leads directly to unsafe `memcpy` on line 5.
parsed_findings.impact: Reliable crash or undefined behavior yields denial-of-service and potential memory corruption depending on platform/ABI.
parsed_findings.proof: With `out==NULL` and `out_sz>0`, control skips the early return and executes `memcpy(out,in,n)` where `n` may be `>0`, writing through a null pointer.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Invoke `copy_name("abc", NULL, 10)` and observe undefined behavior/crash when `memcpy` runs with a null destination and positive length.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The guard `if (out || out_sz == 0)` is inverted, so non-NULL outputs are rejected while NULL destinations can reach `memcpy`.

results.raw_output: {"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-476","file":"safe_copy.c","line_start":3,"line_end":5,"vuln_claim":"The guard `if (out || out_sz == 0)` is inverted, so non-NULL outputs are rejected while NULL destinations can reach `memcpy`.","proof":"With `out==NULL` and `out_sz>0`, control skips the early return and executes `memcpy(out,in,n)` where `n` may be `>0`, writing through a null pointer.","repro_steps":"Invoke `copy_name(\"abc\", NULL, 10)` and observe undefined behavior/crash when `memcpy` runs with a null destination and positive length.","impact":"Reliable crash or undefined behavior yields denial-of-service and potential memory corruption depending on platform/ABI.","confidence":0.95,"chain_steps":"Inverted null check on line 3 leads directly to unsafe `memcpy` on line 5."}]}
